coredata.dc:title: Advanced cement composites: Investigating the role of graphene quantum dots in improving thermal and mechanical performance

coredata.dc:description: The investigation of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) at very small nano- and atomic sizes is a novel approach in the fields of structural cement, concrete, and ceramic materials, which have not been thoroughly explored. This study investigates the comparative effects of two variations of GQDs, Qdl and Qdp, against a control mixture to assess the potential of graphene derivatives in enhancing thermal and mechanical properties of cement composites. The research presents their influences across a wide range of attributes, including flowability, bulk density, permeable voids, water absorption, compressive strength, flexural strength, surface temperature, thermal conductivity, thermal diffusivity, specific heat capacity, Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) isotherm, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), and microstructural characteristics. The empirical data underscore the superior freshness, physical, mechanical, and thermal attributes of GQDs-infused cement composites. Notably, the composites with 0.3 % Qdl show 20.8 % and 50 % increases in compressive and flexural strengths, respectively. On the other hand, the composites with 1.2 % Qdp show significant improvements in both compressive and flexural strengths, being 40 % and 108 % stronger than the control, respectively. Additionally, the thermal properties of the composites exhibit a proportional enhancement with increasing GQDs content. Microstructural analyses reveal a nano-bridging mechanism well-uniformly facilitated by the secondary nucleation and crystallization of calcium silicate hydrate (CSH) gel. This investigation offers innovative insights into the incorporation of GQDs in cement composites, highlighting their potential to create highly conductive structural cement and ceramic.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The investigation of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) at very small nano- and atomic sizes is a novel approach in the fields of structural cement, concrete, and ceramic materials, which have not been thoroughly explored. This study investigates the comparative effects of two variations of GQDs, Qdl and Qdp, against a control mixture to assess the potential of graphene derivatives in enhancing thermal and mechanical properties of cement composites. The research presents their influences across a wide range of attributes, including flowability, bulk density, permeable voids, water absorption, compressive strength, flexural strength, surface temperature, thermal conductivity, thermal diffusivity, specific heat capacity, Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) isotherm, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), and microstructural characteristics. The empirical data underscore the superior freshness, physical, mechanical, and thermal attributes of GQDs-infused cement composites. Notably, the composites with 0.3 % Qdl show 20.8 % and 50 % increases in compressive and flexural strengths, respectively. On the other hand, the composites with 1.2 % Qdp show significant improvements in both compressive and flexural strengths, being 40 % and 108 % stronger than the control, respectively. Additionally, the thermal properties of the composites exhibit a proportional enhancement with increasing GQDs content. Microstructural analyses reveal a nano-bridging mechanism well-uniformly facilitated by the secondary nucleation and crystallization of calcium silicate hydrate (CSH) gel. This investigation offers innovative insights into the incorporation of GQDs in cement composites, highlighting their potential to create highly conductive structural cement and ceramic.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The search for structural cement and ceramic materials that are strong, long-lasting, and environmentally friendly has sparked a wave of creativity, leading to the creation of new additive technologies that improve performance and minimize negative impacts to the environment [1–5]. Nanomaterials and nanotechnology are leading the way in revolutionizing concrete by allowing for the transformation of its properties at the atomic and molecular levels. Recently, many researchers have been adding different types of carbon-based nanomaterials to concrete buildings to make them last longer [6–8]. These include graphene oxide (GO), graphene nanoplatelets, carbon nanotubes, and carbon nanofibers. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), with the crystal less than 5–20 nm in size, are innovative carbon-based materials that have the potential to greatly impact material engineering. Unlike the inorganic quantum dot technology which can yield toxicity to microorganisms and human [9]. They possess a distinctive set of characteristics that arise from the combination of qualities found in both graphene and carbon dots (CDs) [10,11]. GQDs possess remarkable mechanical strength, thermal and electrical conductivity, and hydrophobicity due to their nanoscale size and two-dimensional shape [12]. Using carbon dioxide (CO2) as a carbon source in the green synthesis of GQDs also highlights their ability to have minimal negative effects on the environment, making them a sustainable alternative to traditional additives.

Quantum dots have emerged as a significant development in nanotechnology, finding applications in numerous fields [13]. Sun et al. identified quantum dots as highly suitable photocatalysts for many applications, such as hydrogen generation, CO2 reduction, and pollutant degradation. It also emphasizes the need for further research on stability in complex environments and expanding their industrial applications. In addition, Long et al. [14] examined a novel GQD-assisted ultrasonic dispersion method to enhance the dispersity of metakaolin (MK) in cement composites. The study showed that using GQDs to help the process greatly enhances the dispersity of MK, which results in a 31.03 % rise in the 28-day compressive strength of mortar that contains 1.0 wt% of MK. This method accelerates hydration, boosts C−(A)−S−H formation, and refines the microstructure, offering a cost-effective solution to optimize cement-based materials. He et al. [15] described nitrogen-doped GQDs as a new and inexpensive way to improve the cement's ability to bind chlorides, which makes reinforced concrete (RC) structures last longer. After incorporating 0.2 wt% nitrogen-doped-GQDs, chloride binding increased by 134 % after 14 days of exposure to a 3 M sodium chloride (NaCl) solution. The enhancement is attributed to the increased physical adsorption and slight promotion of chemically bound chloride ions, offering a promising solution for anti-corrosion applications in RC structures. Furthermore, Shan et al. [16] investigated the size-dependent effect of CDs on enhancing the chloride binding capacity of cement composites. The results revealed that smaller CDs (1.73 nm) significantly improved chloride binding by 41 %, when compared with larger CDs (5.79 nm and 12.37 nm), due to increased nucleation sites that promote hydration and the formation of C–S–H gels and monosulfate. These findings highlighted the potential of optimizing CDs sizes to extend the service life of RC structures in chloride-rich environments.

Empirical research has shown that adding GQDs to concrete, specifically at a concentration of 0.3 %, significantly improves mechanical characteristics like compressive and flexural strength. These enhancements range from 10.8 % to 23 %. GQDs also improve the dynamic characteristics and reduce both porosity and depth of chloride penetration, indicating an increase in durability [17,18]. The results emphasize the possibility of using GQDs as environmentally friendly additives to enhance the performance of structural cement and ceramic in thermal and electrical purposes. However, challenges such as limited water solubility and expensive expenses hinder the utilization of carbon nanomaterials like graphene and its derivatives, restricting their feasibility in industrial settings. Qu et al. [19] examined the application of CDs produced via microwave pyrolysis to enhance cement composites. Their analysis validated the successful synthesis of CDs, leading to a 16.8 % increase in compressive strength after 28 days when they used a 0.08 wt% dosage of CDs. This improvement can be attributed to enhanced dispersion, nucleation, and the generation of hydration products. Nanoparticles develop the thermal possessions of cement by making it more compact, resulting in better insulation and heat transport compared to standard cements [20]. According to a reference [21], adding graphene to cement at a weight percentage of 0.25 % significantly improves thermal conductivity. The features and structures of nanoparticles play a critical role in modulating the thermal conductivity of concrete. As a result, it is essential to employ effective methodologies in the selection of suitable nanomaterials to improve concrete's thermal performance.

Previous studies [17,22] successfully made GQDs and used their unique characteristics to make cementitious materials perform better in both fresh and hardened states. This demonstrated the potential of GQDs as innovative and environmentally friendly additives in the structural cement and ceramic industry. Despite the exceptional characteristics of GQDs, their potential in cement composites remains largely unexplored, providing ample opportunities for further research and advancement. Many academic studies have explored the use of GQDs in concrete, uncovering encouraging findings such as enhanced dispersibility, faster hydration reactions, and greater mechanical strength. Nevertheless, the full utilization of GQDs has not yet been realized [23–25]. Additional research is necessary in order to completely unleash their transformational potential.

The goal of this paper is to examine the multifaceted impact of GQDs on various attributes of cement composites. The following properties are included: the ability to flow, the density, the number of pores, the ability to absorb water, the compressive strength and flexural strengths, the surface temperature, the thermal conductivity, the thermal diffusivity, the specific heat capacity, the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) isotherm analysis, the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), and the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) coupled with energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). This research endeavors to comprehensively evaluate the mechanical, long-term durability, and functional characteristics of GQDs in relation to concrete technology. It investigates the processes involved in the creation and integration of GQDs, closely examining their overall structure and potential advantages in improving concrete performance and sustainability. Furthermore, our inquiry focuses on studying the interactions between GQDs and cementitious matrices, and then understanding how these interactions influence the microstructure and characteristics of concrete. Our goal is to clarify the fundamental mechanisms that control these interactions, therefore making it easier to incorporate GQDs as a crucial component in environmentally friendly, high-performance concrete structures. Therefore, this study aims to fill the research gap in state-of-the-art knowledge concerning the use of GQDs in concrete, laying the groundwork for future advancements in this domain.

Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC) Type I, adhering to ASTM C150 [26] standards, was employed. This cement features a specific gravity of 3.15 and a Blaine surface area of 3862 cm2/g. The chemical composition of OPC was revealed using X-ray fluorescence (XRF) analysis, implemented with a Bruker S8 Tiger instrument. The fine aggregate employed was premium-quality natural sand, meeting ASTM C33 [27] specifications. This sand exhibits a specific gravity of 2.67, a fineness modulus of 2.91, and a water absorption rate of 0.63 %. Fig. 1 presents the particle size distribution of this fine aggregate. Tables 1 and 2
provide a detailed overview of the chemical compositions, specifications, and characteristics of the raw materials used in the experiments. The study assessed two synthesized variants of GQDs. The first variant, designated as “Qdl,” is in liquid form, while the second, “Qdp,” consists of a suprastructure of Qdl assemblies in a fine powder form. The GQDs synthesized in this study are indeed distinct from traditional quantum dots, and their properties align more closely with graphene-like structures. The dark color observed in the liquid form is due to the post-synthesis treatment of GQDs at 600 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere, which induces the formation of a “suprastructure of GQDs assemblies”. This process transforms the GQDs into sp2-hybridized carbon allotropes, including graphene-like nanosheets and other 2D and 3D carbon structures. Regarding the SEM images, while they may appear to show graphene-like fragments rather than distinct quantum dots, this is consistent with the morphology of the synthesized GQDs, which include a variety of graphene-derived structures due to the post-synthesis treatment. Although the SEM images might not fully capture the quantum dot nature due to resolution limitations, the size of the GQDs remains in the nanometer scale as intended. As for the elemental composition, while carbon constitutes 64.0 % of the GQD powder, the remaining elements are likely residuals from the synthesis process. These remaining elements may alter the cement properties although a very small amount of GQDs is only used. The observed improvements in material properties are due to the GQDs' inherent characteristics, such as quantum confinement effects, non-zero band gap, and edge effects, rather than the presence of non-carbon elements. Both GQDs types had been sourced from CrystalLyte Company, Bangkok, Thailand. Fig. 2 presents the synthesized GQDs, showcasing the distinct forms under evaluation. Fig. 3 shows the micrographs of Qdp using SEM-EDX at 100 magnification level.

The quantities of materials for seven different mixing proportions were carefully prepared in accordance with the requirements as per ASTM C109 [28]. The combinations consisted of different proportions of Qdl and Qdp as additives to the composites. In addition, a control mixture having water-to-cement ratio (w/c) of 0.4, was created without Qdl and Qdp for the purpose of comparison investigation. The Qdl and Qdp were initially added to the dry cement powder at concentrations of 0 %, 0.3 %, 0.6 %, 0.9 %, and 1.2 % relative to the weight of OPC having the w/c of 0.4. A label clearly identified each mixture, indicating the precise content of Qdl and Qdp. For example, mix Qdl-0.3 contained 0.3 % Qdl, while mix Qdp-1.2 contained 1.2 % Qdp. The mixtures were carefully created in a controlled laboratory environment for thorough experimental examination. Moreover, OPC composite pastes, containing both Qdl and Qdp as well as those without, were developed in order to examine their thermal characteristics and changes in microstructure. The preparation of these pastes followed the same procedure as the mortar mixtures, with the exception that fine aggregates were not included. Table 3 specifies the specific ratios of the raw components used in these mixes.

The first phase of the experimental protocol entailed precise quantification and consolidation of the unique components within each blend. To achieve optimal dispersion of Qdp particles and mitigate agglomeration, a systematic approach was implemented. A total of 500 g of the mixing water and the superplasticizer were combined in a beaker. Next, the Qdp particles were introduced into the solution and stirred manually with a rod until they were fully dissolved. Afterwards, a 60 Hz (280 W) ultrasonicator was used to subject the suspension to sonication for a duration of 1 h, which led to the achievement of a uniform and homogeneous dispersion of particles. The specimens were mixed and cured according to the parameters outlined in ASTM C305 [29]. It is important to mention that the dispersion phases were not carried out for Qdl. During the preparation process, meticulous measurement and the introduction of OPC and fine aggregate into a mechanical mixer was performed. Theamalgamation of these two constituents persisted for approximately 1 min. Following this, the residual water for blending and the particle suspension, encompassing water, Qdp, and superplasticizer, were mixed until achieving a uniform consistency in the fresh mixture. The complete mixing procedure lasted around 3 min. After achieving a uniform mortar mixture, an assessment was conducted to evaluate its qualities while it was still fresh. The workability was evaluated by conducting a flow table test following the specifications provided in ASTM C1437 [30]. After assessing the freshly prepared mortar, it was molded and exposed to a carefully regulated curing process in an atmosphere with a temperature of 23 ± 2 °C and a relative humidity of 95 % for a duration of 24 h before being taken out of the molds. Following the removal of the molds, the samples were placed in a curing tank that contained fresh water to get additional curing before being examined. The tests were conducted based on triplicate samples to guarantee precision and consistency.

The schematic representation of material preparation and experimental processes can be found in Figs. 4–7
. Table 4 provides an extensive summary of the specific methodologies used in this investigation. The solidified composites were thoroughly analyzed for several parameters, including total density, volume of permeable voids (VPV), water absorption capacity, compressive and flexural strengths, surface temperature, thermal conductivity, thermal diffusivity, and specific heat capacity. The evaluation utilized 50-mm mortar cubes to measure the bulk density after 28 days, the VPV after 28 days, the water absorption after 28 days, and the compressive strengths after 7 and 28 days. The 40 mm × 40 mm × 160 mm specimens were prepared for 7- and 28-day flexural strength tests. A surface temperature evaluation was performed on 28-day-hardened mortar samples with the sizes of 300 mm × 300 mm × 50 mm. The surface temperature was monitored at nine specific points using a thermographic camera (FLIR C3-X, Estonia) every hour for 72 h, as shown in Fig. 6. In order to facilitate the assessment of thermal conductivity, specimens with dimensions of 25 mm × 3 mm × 25 mm were prepared. Subsequently, a TPS thermal conductivity analyzer (BAXIT, Shanghai, China) was used to determine the thermal conductivity of 28-day-hardened paste samples with different dosages of GQDs. While doing the measurement, the sensor was placed between two samples, and Parker's formula [31] was utilized to establish the thermal diffusivity of the composite samples.

where α represents thermal diffusivity in mm2/s; t1/2 is the time in seconds to reach 50 % of the maximum temperature amplitude; and d is the thickness of the material in meters across the heat flow direction. The average values were reported based on three tests.

BET isotherm approach was used to evaluate the adsorption characteristics of different materials [32]. The micro-pore characteristics of cement composites were studied, which is directly associated with their strength. The samples underwent a vacuum treatment, resulting in the adsorption of nitrogen gas by the cement matrix. The adsorbed nitrogen amount and the corresponding adsorption capabilities were determined using a Micromeritics 3Flex device (Micromeritics Device Corporation, USA) using nitrogen adsorption measurements. To analyze the pores’ micro-structures, 1 g of the sample that had been cured for 28 days was employed. The analysis covered pore diameters ranging from 1 to 500 nm, as predicted by the BET theory. The pore size distribution results were then reported.

In addition, powdered samples obtained from specimens that were cured for 28 days were analyzed using TGA with the PerkinElmer Pyris 1 equipment (USA) to determine the phase formation. The TGA methodology involved gradually increasing the temperature of the samples from 25 to 1000 °C at a rate of 10 °C per minute. This was done while maintaining a constant flow of nitrogen gas at a rate of 70 ml/min.

The microstructural morphology examination of the cement paste composites was performed using SEM-EDS (SUPRA 66VP instrument, Oberkochen, Germany). The SEM device enabled the analysis of the microscopic structures of specimens at the atomic level. Before testing, the fractured areas of cement composite pastes were coated with gold in order to improve electrical conductivity. Afterwards, the samples that had been coated were placed into the SEM apparatus, and images were taken for a duration of 6000 revolutions. The SEM images used in this study depict frequently seen morphologies during the examination. Finally, the Raman spectroscopy analysis was performed to assess the carbon characteristics in cement matrix. The excitation laser was 532 nm; objective lens of 50×; Raman mapping of 200 × 200 μm with 1 μm/pixel; and accumulation time of 0.23 s per point.

Fig. 8 (a) depicts the flowability results of properly mixed mortars. The flow values of mortars, which included Qdl, varied from 99 % to 115 %. This suggests that the mortars' workability improved as the Qdl content increased in comparison to the control mix. Specifically, as the Qdl level went up from 0.3 % to 1.2 %, the workability of both combinations presented a respective improvement of 10 % and 28 % compared to the control mixture. Similar to the Qdl mixture, mortars with Qdp exhibited higher flow values. However, when the Qdp content increased, there was a minor decline. The findings are consistent with the findings reported by Tanvir et al. [35]. Notably, the newly prepared mortar with the highest Qdp concentration (1.2 %) had a flow value that was 28 % higher than the standard sample. Nevertheless, the occurrence of Qdp had a small effect on the capacity of mortars to flow, even when Qdp contents were as high as 1.2 %. In general, all of the newly prepared mixtures exhibited good flowability characteristics. Out of all the options, the freshly made mortar with 0.3 % Qdp showed the greatest degree of flowability, reaching a level of 120 %.

The inherent hydrophilic nature of Qdp, further enhanced by the addition of a superplasticizer to the Qdp solution, accounts for the enhanced workability of mortars containing Qdp. The superplasticizer improved the dispersion of the fresh mortars, resulting in a consistent enhancement of their flowability. The differences in their structure and handling process during composite preparation contribute to the positive influence of Qdp on flowability compared to Qdl. Due to the solid state of Qdp, it should be better to distribute it uniformly within the cementitious matrix and prevent particles from clustering together. In order to accomplish this, it was necessary to employ a dispersion method with a superplasticizer additive based on polycarboxylate ether (PCE). This technique also improves the ability to flow smoothly. In contrast, the Qdl liquid state makes it easier to integrate without the need for dispersion. This results in a higher water-to-binder (w/b) ratio of 0.55 compared to the Qdp samples, which have a w/b ratio of 0.42. The increased Qdl ratio allowed for their integration without compromising their workability. Researchers have used superplasticizers to acquire the workability of cementitious composites that include graphene nanoparticles [36,37].

Table 5 presents the physical attributes of all specimens that were subjected to a 28-day period of curing. Fig. 8(b) depicts the bulk density of cement composites containing varying quantities of Qdl and Qdp after 28 days. The findings showed that composites with 0.3 % and 1.2 % Qdl had density improvements of about 3.5 % and 2.1 %, respectively. The 0.3 % Qdl composite demonstrated the greatest density, determining 2172.1 kg/m3. After 28 days, using Qdl at concentrations greater than 0.3 % resulted in a smaller reduction in the bulk density of the mortars. Additionally, the densities of the Qdp samples after a period of 28 days exhibited a range of values between 2179.2 and 2201.3 kg/m3. The densities of Qdp samples (Qdp-0.3, Qdp-0.6, Qdp-0.9, and Qdp-1.2) were 3.9 %, 4.0 %, 4.4 %, and 4.9 %, respectively, higher than the density of the control. Moreover, the control composite had a comparatively low density, but the Qdp-1.2 had the highest density. This 28-day density trend aligns with the detected mechanical characteristics of cement composites, which encompass Qdl and Qdp. These Qdl and Qdp can fill in the gaps in the cement matrix. This makes the interfacial transition zone denser. This compaction process subsequently improves the mechanical robustness of the composite material [38,39].

Fig. 8(c) demonstrates the impact of Qdl and Qdp contents on the VPV in 28-day hardened cement composites. The results suggest that a rise in Qdl concentration from 0.3 % to 1.2 % results in a notable reduction in VPV levels. Specifically, when compared to the control sample, the VPV of the Qdl-0.3 mixture decreased by 42.9 %, while the VPV of the Qdl-1.2 mixture decreased by 32.5 %. The inclusion of 0.3 % Qdl yielded the highest level of resistance to water permeability; however, a concentration of 1.2 % Qdl caused a reduction in water permeability resistance. Nevertheless, all Qdl-cement composite mixes exhibited reduced VPV values in comparison to the control. The addition of 0.3 % Qdl led to the most significant decrease in VPV after 28-days curing, surpassing the control mixture. The decrease in VPV seen in Qdl-cement composites is most likely attributed to their filling action. Adding Qdl enhances the pore-refined structure, diminishes overall porosity, and augments the compactness of cement-based materials. As a result, this results in an enhancement of the mechanical characteristics of the materials.

The results propose that Qdp has a positive impact on the composites' VPV in comparison to the control mixture. The VPV values of the 0.3–1.2 % Qdp samples were 46.1 %, 48.7 %, 49.4 %, and 51.3 % lower than the control, respectively. The control exhibited one of the highest values for VPV, whereas Qdp-1.2 composite demonstrated the lowest VPV. The data noted from prior research [38], indicating that the Qdp-1.2 mixture exhibits VPV values that were roughly 39 % lower than those of the Qdl-1.2 solution. This suggests that the Qdp-composites contains much fewer permeable voids compared to the Qdl-composites. The fewer permeable cavities indicate that more compact matrix yields enhanced durability. The use of Qdl and Qdp improves the cement hydration process by consistently producing hydration products that fill their micropores. The inclusion of graphene compounds in the OPC structure only results in their chemical interaction with PCE, without any impact on the microstructural CSH gel. According to findings from SEM micrographs, the expedited hydration reaction of cement composites is responsible for the decrease in their VPV value.

Fig. 8(d) illustrates the results of the water absorption experiment performed on 28-day cement composites containing Qdl and Qdp. The results suggest that adding Qdl and Qdp to the composites reduces water absorption. The mixes, including Qdl-0.3 and Qdl-1.2, exhibited reductions of 37.1 % and 22.6 %, respectively, compared to the control mix. The mixture with a concentration of 0.3 % Qdl showed the smallest water absorption value, while raising the concentration to 1.2 % resulted in higher absorption values. Nevertheless, all Qdl-cement composite mixes demonstrated lower water absorption data, when compared with the control. The adding 0.3 % Qdl led to the most significant decrease in water absorption, resulting in a reduction of nearly 37 %, when compared with the control mixture. Moreover, the Qdp-1.2 composites showed the lowest level of the 28-day water absorption. The water absorption values for the 0.3–1.2 % Qdp composites demonstrated obviously lower values, when compared with the control. These reductions of the 0.3–1.2 % Qdp composites at approximately 38.7 %, 40.3 %, 41.9 %, and 45.2 % in water absorption, respectively, indicating a significant improvement in water resistance. The improved secondary-nucleation effect of these graphene derivatives, which promotes the formation of the CSH gel and diminishes the number of pores in the cement matrix's microstructure, is responsible for the decrease in water absorption [40]. As a result, the microstructure of the concrete and its interfacial transition zone becomes densified, yielding less porous and less water-absorbed products. The findings of the water absorption test are consistent with the results obtained from the VPV test. As previously noted, the rate of water absorption is predominantly influenced by capillary pores. Hence, the decrease in the absorption coefficient in Qdl and Qdp composites can be ascribed to the increase in small microstructural voids and the decrease in capillary porosity.

Fig. 9 illustrates a strong correlation (R2 = 0.99) between water absorption and VPV values, indicating a positive relationship between these two variables. Consequently, it may be inferred that higher concentrations of Qdl (at 0.3 %) and Qdp (at 1.2 %) will affect diminished water absorption, aligning with the VPV results. Capillary porosity and surface energy primarily influence water absorption, a widely acknowledged fact. Prior research has emphasized the capacity of graphene nanoparticles to improve the dispersion of pores in cementitious materials [41].

Table 6 displays the mechanical characteristics of all samples that underwent 7- and 28-day curing period. Fig. 10 (a) illustrates the 7- and 28-day compressive strengths of hardened cement composites with varying Qdl and Qdp dosages. The findings demonstrate a steady and uniform rise in compressive strengths for all mixtures, regardless of the presence of GQDs, as time progresses. The continuing hydration reactions within the cement matrix primarily cause this rise. Significantly, Qdl mixes exhibited progressive improvement in strength over time, resulting in a substantial growth in overall strength. For instance, the Qdl-0.3 mix exhibited 7- and 28-day compressive strengths that were approximately 68.5 % and 20.8 % greater than the control, respectively. Nevertheless, when the Qdl content reached 1.2 %, the compressive strengths exhibited a progressive decline over time, aligning with the observations completed by Qu et al. [19]. This suggests that as Qdl concentrations increase, the compressive strength also increases. However, there comes a point at which the effectiveness of Qdl starts to decrease. The observed decrease in performance with increasing Qdl concentration from 0.3 % to 1.2 % can be attributed to several factors. At higher concentrations, such as 1.2 %, Qdl may tend to aggregate or form clusters due to increased intermolecular interactions, which reduces their effective surface area and overall performance. Additionally, there is often an optimal concentration of additives that maximizes performance, and exceeding this optimal level can lead to diminished benefits. Furthermore, higher concentrations can pose challenges in achieving uniform dispersion within the matrix, leading to non-uniform properties. These factors collectively suggest that the 0.3 % concentration may be closer to the optimal amount for the specific application, while the higher concentration results in less effective performance. However, their compressive strengths still remained considerably higher than those of the control.

The inclusion of Qdp in the mixtures led to increased compressive strengths across all compositions, with values ranging from 0.3 % to 1.2 %, as illustrated in Fig. 10(b). The 28-day composites containing 0.3 %, 0.6 %, 0.9 %, and 1.2 % Qdp demonstrated compressive strengths that were 15.1 %, 21.3 %, 24.2 %, and 39.2 %, respectively, higher than the control. The research accompanied by Long et al. and Qu et al. [14,19] is consistent with these findings. The Qdp-1.2 mixture exhibited a notable enhancement of 39.2 % in 28-day compressive strength, reaching 62.8 MPa, when compared to the control. After 28 days, incorporating Qdp led to a significant 39.2 % improvement in compressive strength, surpassing the strength gains investigated by Qu et al. [19] and Li et al. [42]. The existing study demonstrated a more substantial improvement in compressive strength compared to previous research [18], with increases of up to 40 % using 1.2 % Qdp. It introduces a novel nano-bridging mechanism driven by secondary nucleation and crystallization of CSH gel, which significantly enhances the compressive strength. This deeper understanding of microstructural changes marks a significant advancement in optimizing GQDs content for superior cement composite performance.

In terms of compressive strength, the presence of Qdp can expedite the initial stage of strength growth, specifically observed after 7 days. The compressive strengths observed after 28 days of aging are comparable to those attained when utilizing GO [43]. It can be attributed the accelerated early-age strength improvement with Qdp to its role as a smaller filler and nucleation site. Section 3.7 will provide further discussion on this topic, with a specific focus on microstructural examination. Li and Shi [44] achieved comparable findings by employing GO to enhance the strength of concrete. Research [45] indicates that incorporating GO can result in a minimum 30 % improvement in the 28-day compressive strength. Nevertheless, GO, likely homophobic material, is comparatively costly, requires a more intricate preparation procedure, and demonstrates inferior dispersibility in cement-based materials, when compared to Qdp [46,47]. Therefore, homogeneity and uniformity for particle dispersion of the fresh mixtures can be the key influence the performance of GO composites. Qdp has many functional groups that make it better at chemically reacting with cement hydrates. Its small size also makes it easier for it to get into the spaces between cement particles, which increases the compressive strength. The results show that adding GQDs to OPC in the form of a powder greatly raises the compressive strength of concrete. The compressive strength of Qdp-1.2 is approximately 27.9 % greater than that of the Qdl solution.

In addition, the addition of Qdl and Qdp to cement led to a reduction in VPV and water absorption values, while simultaneously enhancing compressive strengths. The graphene derivatives consumed a greater amount of quartz (SiO2), mullite (Al2O3), and tricalcium aluminate (C3A) from the mixture constituents, as well as portlandite from the hydration products, leading to the increase in strength. This led to an improvement in the hydration reactions, leading to the production of larger quantities of CSH and calcium aluminate silicate hydrate (CASH) gels, which in turn resulted in higher levels of strength. Hong et al. [43] conducted an investigation and found similar results, attributing the increased strength of GO-modified concrete to enhanced hydration processes. The incorporation of oxygen atoms into the functional groups attached to GO did not immediately alter the hydrated crystals. Nevertheless, it influenced the hydration process by attracting the tricalcium silicate (C3S), dicalcium silicate (C2S), and tricalcium aluminate (C3A) phases. These phases subsequently served as secondary nucleation sites for the creation of hydrated products [48]. Consequently, the cement hydration process was significantly influenced by a large number of functional oxygen groups, resulting in the creation of a densely packed, linked network of crystalline microstructure and ultimately increasing compressive strength in hardened composites. Moreover, the electronegativity of graphene derivatives facilitates the attraction of positive ion, such as Ca2+, Na+, and K+, into the matrix. These cations underwent a chemical transformation when reacted with alkali (Al), silica (Si), and sulfur trioxide (SO3), resulting in the promotion of CSH, CASH, and ettringite (Aft) phase formation. The formation of these phases with water molecules contributed to the development of a more compact structure and the area where different materials interact, thereby refining their resulting crystals.

Fig. 10(c) presents the 7- and 28-day flexural strengths of all mixtures, including Qdl and Qdp. Tested results indicate a steady rise in flexural strengths as time continues, which can be attributed to the progression of hydration reactions, reflecting the results observed for compressive strength. The Qdl and Qdp composites, at all concentrations evaluated, exhibited superior performance compared to the control mixture. These findings are consistent with the compressive strength values presented in Fig. 10(a). Cement composites with Qdl concentrations ranging from 0.3 % to 1.2 % exhibited higher flexural strengths, compared to the control. This finding is advocated by previous research [40,43], that has also shown that graphene-based materials improve flexural strength in concrete mixes compared to normal materials. The control mix exhibited flexural strengths of 5.3 MPa and 7.2 MPa at 7 and 28 days, respectively. The 0.3 % Qdl mix showed the most significant improvements in flexural strengths, with values of 8.3 MPa at 7 days and 10.8 MPa at 28 days. These data indicate an approximate increase of 57 % and 50 % compared to the control mix after 7 and 28 days, respectively. The Qdl-1.2 mix exhibited a flexural strength that was approximately 38 % greater than the control at 7 days and 32 % higher at 28 days. Prior investigations [49,50] noted that the nano-bridging effects of graphene derivatives, which function as reinforcement akin to microfibers, yielding the enhancements in their flexural strengths.

In addition, the flexural strengths exhibited a steady increase as the Qdp concentration increased, as exhibited in Fig. 10(d). Results revealed that the flexural strengths of Qdp at concentrations of 0.3 %, 0.6 %, and 0.9 % were about 69.4 %, 87.5 %, and 95.8 % higher than control, respectively. The Qdp-1.2 mix demonstrated 90.6 % and 108.3 % greater in 7- and 28-day flexural strengths than the control. The addition of graphene derivatives greatly improved the flexural strength of the cement composites, in line with the reported improvement in compressive strength achieved with Qdl and Qdp. Both the 7-day and 28-day composites revealed a notable disparity in flexural strength between Qdp-1.2 and Qdl-1.2. After 28 days, the Qdp-1.2 composite exhibited 57.9 % greater in flexural strength than the Qdl-1.2 one.

This suggests that adding the suprastructure of Qdl assemblies to cement composites promotes strong bonding between particles and the cement matrix at the interface. The Qdp sheet-like structures act as microstructural reinforcement, effectively reducing the deflection of microcracks and impeding the propagation of cracks. As a result, they enhance both the resistance to bending and the compressive strength [51,52]. When graphene derivatives and the cement matrix come into contact, they establish a robust covalent connection at the interface. When external pressures that cause bending, the fragile cement matrix transfers these forces to the bending-resistant Qdp sheets. This leads to significant enhancements in the bending properties of the GQDs composites. As a result, the Qdl assemblies' superstructure significantly improves both their ability to withstand compression and their resistance to bending in cement composites. It should be noted that the structural cement composites typically have weak bending performance. To incorporating with these GQDs, this may broaden a new possibility to reduce or remove steel bar in reinforced concrete. This yields to reduce material cost and avoid steel corrosion effect.

In summary, the study illustrates the distinct effects of Qdl and Qdp on the mechanical characteristics of cement composites. Over a period of time, both Qdl and Qdp greatly improved the compressive and flexural strengths, with Qdp exhibiting a more noticeable impact. The compressive strength of Qdl mixes exhibited a significant initial rise, especially at lower doses, however, reached a plateau and slightly decreased at higher dosages. Qdp demonstrated sustained enhancement in compressive strength, with a notable 39.2 % increase at a concentration of 1.2 % after 28 days. This improvement exceeded the performance of Qdl by 27.9 %. The findings regarding flexural strength exhibited comparable patterns. Over a period of 28 days, Qdp demonstrated superior performance compared to Qdl, exhibiting a remarkable improvement of up to 108.3 % at a concentration of 1.2 %. In contrast, Qdl only displayed a modest improvement of 57.9 %. Notably, while previous studies [18,22,53] have demonstrated the potential of GQDs and related nanomaterials in enhancing the mechanical and thermal properties of cement composites, our research presents a novel approach by evaluating the effects of two distinct variations of GQDs (Qdl and Qdp). For instance, the Qdp used in our study resulted in up to 108.3 % improvement in flexural strength, a significant enhancement compared to the 50 % improvement observed in similar studies [18]. The exceptional performance of Qdp can be related to its compact size and enhanced dispersibility, which enable more efficient incorporation into the cement matrix and enhance the reinforcing of the atomic structures. This leads to a denser and more interconnected arrangement of crystals, which improves the mechanical characteristics of the material. The findings emphasize the potential of Qdp as a cost-effective and efficient additive for significantly improving the strength and durability of structural cement and ceramic innovation, especially in energy-harvesting and smart city projects.

After being cured for 28 days, Fig. 11 (a)–(d) show the surface temperature, thermal conductivity, thermal diffusivity, and specific heat capacity of cement composites with different GQDs. The surface temperature distribution on the surface of GQDs composites was observed for 72 h using infrared thermal imaging, as depicted in Fig. 6. The control sample had a surface temperature measurement at the maximum of 38 °C at the middle part, whereas the Qdl-1.2 composite had the maximum temperature of 41 °C and the Qdp-1.2 composite had the maximum temperature value of 44 °C. Out of all the composites made using GQDs and cement, the Qdp-1.2 composite showed the greatest increase in temperature, as shown in Fig. 11(a). Fig. 11(b) illustrates the lowest thermal conductivity of control mix, compared to GQDs-cement composites, which explains the observed temperature increase in Qdl and Qdp composites. However, the inclusion of Qdp consistently demonstrated a rise in surface temperature. These results show how GQDs nanosheets interact with the CSH and CH phases in cement mortar to change the thermal properties of GQD-cement composites [22]. Therefore, the incorporation of GQD-cement composites offers an alternative system to greatly improve their rate of heat transmission.

Table 7 displays the thermal characteristics of all samples that cured for 28 days. The results revealed significant improvements in the compressive and flexural strengths of hardened cement composites with the addition of Qdl and Qdp. This part also showcases the thermal conductivity measurements of cement composites, which encompass Qdl and Qdp, as illustrated in Fig. 11(b). The thermal conductivity values of these GQDs exhibited a consistent improvement across all mixes. More precisely, the 0.3 % and 1.2 % Qdl composites showed a substantial difference in conductivity that was higher than the control. Nevertheless, increasing the Qdl dosages to 1.2 % resulted in a minor decrease in thermal conductivity. The results showed that the thermal conductivity of cement pastes slightly dropped as the amount of Qdl grew, whereas it dramatically increased with a larger Qdp content. As a result, GQDs have a considerable impact on the heat conductivity of cement pastes. Significantly, composite pastes with GQDs exhibited superior thermal conductivity compared to pure pastes lacking GQDs. The change can be attributed to the intrinsic property of graphene to conduct heat efficiently [54]. The uniform dispersion of graphene in the cement creates a network that enhances heat transfer. In addition, an increased number of microstructural pores leads to higher air volumes within the structure, resulting in reduced heat conductivity caused by the presence of air cavities, which will be discussed in the following section.

In addition, the thermal conductivity values for the Qdp-0.3, Qdp-0.6, Qdp-0.9, and Qdp-1.2 composites were determined to be 0.341, 0.396, 0.412, and 0.529 W/m·K, respectively. The Qdp-1.2 composite demonstrated a substantial enhancement in heat conductivity, showing an estimated 69 % rise in comparison to the control. The increased heat conductivity suggests that GQD-composites have great potential as alternative products for thermal cement and ceramic. Therefore, the use of GQDs in cement and ceramic used in structural applications has the potential to improve sustainability and energy efficiency. Cui et al. [55], likewise, confirmed the findings of this investigation regarding the heat conductivity of cement mortar enhanced with graphene. Incorporating GQDs into the cement composites greatly improves the thermal diffusivity of the composite. Therefore, by minimizing the temperature gradient resulting from cement hydration in large buildings, the likelihood of thermal cracking can be decreased while simultaneously improving the thermal stability and longevity of the structures [31]. The investigation revealed a positive correlation between the quantity of GQDs and the thermal conductivity of the composites, proving graphene's capacity to effectively disperse heat. Jiang et al. [50] and Gholami and Jalilisadrabad [56] that this feature was especially beneficial for unique dwellings, buildings, and pavements in frigid areas, as solar heat can effectively warm the buildings and pavements during daylight hours, hence decreasing the need for heating energy.

Integrating high thermal diffusivity into cement-based materials offers essential capabilities, including structures that can melt ice and snow, pavements, and ceramic tiles that can carry electricity, and the ability to monitor the health of corroded concrete structures for the future of “Smart City” [57]. Thermal diffusivity, a property that encompasses both thermal conductivity and heat capacity, enables efficient and swift movement and dispersion of heat, making it highly useful in the process of melting ice and snow. Materials with high thermal diffusivity can be intentionally designed to also have enhanced electrical conductivity, making them beneficial for use in conductive pavements. Despite their primary function of facilitating heat transfer, it is possible to manufacture these materials with this additional property. In addition, high thermal diffusivity can enhance the efficiency of sensors integrated into concrete for the purpose of monitoring its state. This is because variations in thermal characteristics can serve as indicators of structural integrity problems, such as corrosion.

Fig. 11 (c) displays the thermal diffusivity characteristics of cement composites that incorporate GQDs. When 0.3 % and 1.2 % Qdl were added, the resistivity greatly dropped. This caused their thermal diffusivities to rise by about 125 % and 110 %, respectively, compared to the control. This suggests that adding Qdl to cement composites improves thermal diffusivity. The results of the Qdp composites indicate that the 0.3 % Qdp composite has lower thermal diffusivity value than the rest, and results in a 40 % increase in thermal diffusivity, when compared with the control. The use of GQDs in cement composites clearly improves their thermal characteristics. When the composite contains 1.2 % Qdp, the thermal diffusivity increases significantly. The increases in diffusivity observed with GQDs addition indicate a strong likelihood of displaying semiconducting characteristics in terms of thermal properties. This, in turn, creates several possibilities for utilizing GQDs composites in various thermal and structural applications.

The findings suggest the potential use of GQD-cement composites in the structural and electrical infrastructure, including ceramic coverings, pavements, walls, columns, and girders. Percolation theory states that the resistivity of composite media is not directly proportional to the quantity of conductive particles present. However, a notable and abrupt shift in resistivity occurs at the percolation threshold, leading to the stabilization of resistivity. Within this framework, the cement composites can be examined by applying the parallel and series model of composite theory, which treats it as a network of resistances [58]. Consequently, the high thermal conductivity of graphene nanomaterials often enhances their thermal diffusivity. This enhancement can result in superior heat management characteristics in cement and ceramic composites, which is advantageous for diverse applications where thermal properties are critical.

The specific heat capacity values for the control, Qdl-0.3, Qdl-1.2, Qdp-0.3, Qdp-0.6, Qdp-0.9, and Qdp-1.2 composites in Fig. 11(d) were measured as 0.063, 0.115, 0.093, 0.087, 0.107, 0.204, and 0.244 J/gK, respectively. The addition of GQDs greatly increased the heat capacity, resulting in an 83 % improvement at Qdl-0.3 and around 48 % at Qdl-1.2, compared to the control. The Qdp-0.3 composite displayed the lowest specific heat capacity, compared to the other GQD composites. Increasing the Qdp concentration led to an increase in the heat capacity, likely due to the significant thermal conductivity of GQD sheets and their effective interaction with the CSH gel and CH phases. Significant heat conductivity was seen at 0.9 % (Qdp-0.9) and 1.2 % (Qdp-1.2) GQDs content, which is in line with the excellent thermal characteristics observed in GO composites [22]. The addition of GQD to the cement composites resulted in significant alterations to the paste's microstructural, morphological, and thermal characteristics. The composites exhibited an increase in surface temperature, thermal conductivity, thermal diffusivity, and specific heat capacity as the GQD contents rose. This improvement is especially vital during the exothermic reactions that take place in the early stages of OPC hydration. The interactions between the CSH and CH phases in the cement matrix and GQDs significantly influence the heat transfer properties of the GQDs composites. The material's thermal parameters exhibit an optimal range of thermal conductivity, ranging from 0.313 to 0.529 W/m·K, thermal diffusivity from 0.720 to 1.786 mm2/s, and specific heat capacity from 0.063 to 0.244 J/g·K. These values indicate that the material has the potential to effectively transmit heat. The findings of this study indicate that cement composites could be extremely advantageous for heat storage applications in the future.

Table 8 provides a summary of overview of the influence of the presence of Qdl and Qdp in cement composites on their thermal characteristics. The summary indicated that the utilization of graphene derivatives resulted in improved freshness and mechanical properties. Qdp composites had exceptional thermal characteristics, whereas Qdl composites exhibited only minor enhancements. On the other hand, the control composites show neither similar mechanical nor thermal performance. The investigations show that the GQDs assemblies provide novel material for the development of structural and conductive composites for use in the future applications.

The relationship between the porosity of the cement matrix and the strength of GQDs composites is determined by assessing the volume of pores presenting in these composites. Greater porosity indicates a lower-density internal arrangement, thereby reducing the composite's strength. This is especially critical in reinforced concrete, as deleterious elements that cause reinforcement corrosion can infiltrate through these tiny openings, negatively affecting the composites' durability and performance [59–62]. Fig. 12 (a) depicts the microscopic pore volume of the Qdl and Qdp composites. The results suggest that adding both Qdl and Qdp to the cement leads to a significant drop in pore volume, compared to the control. As the quantity of GQDs increases, the volume of medium-sized pores decreases. The synergistic interactions of GQDs, which restrict the creation of pores, are responsible for the observed impact.

Furthermore, the results indicate that Qdp has a smaller dV/dw pore volume compared to the Qdl solution across the size range of 1.7–211 nm. This indicates that adding Qdp to cement can efficiently reduce nano-, meso-, and micro-pores, resulting in more compact cement structures in comparison to GQDs in their liquid state. Table 9 shows that Qdp-1.2 has a 30 % lower pore volume than Qdl-1.2. Furthermore, the surface area examination reveals that the control combination has a significantly greater surface area than both Qdl-1.2 and Qdp-1.2. Qdp inclusion facilitates the formation of cement composites with more refined pore architectures, resulting in improved overall density, yielded from secondary nucleation effects, as aforementioned. The findings suggest that incorporating Qdp into cement-based materials has the dual effect of enhancing the material's density while also improving pore structure and reducing porosity. These alterations in structure lead to the observed enhancements in mechanical properties. Besides, the integration of Qdp has a positive influence on both individual and large-scale characteristics of the cement and ceramic composites, resulting in improved robustness and sustainability.

Because thermal energy has a big effect on the breakdown of GQDs [63], GQDs in their normal state are likely to break down into gaseous substances during the highly exothermic hydration reaction. This stops the strength from getting better. In contrast, the Qdp structure exhibits a considerably higher decomposition temperature, as seen by the TGA measurements conducted in a nitrogen atmosphere (Fig. 12(b)). These data reveal a weight loss of less than 30 % even at a temperature as high as 1000 °C. The enhanced thermal stability of GQDs prevents their disintegration due to the localized heat generated during hydration. While the hydration reactions progress, the heat is generated by cement hydration, leading to the break-down of the covalent and non-covalent bonds within GQDs clusters. This is different from how GQDs normally break down into gaseous products. The heat stability and breakdown characteristics of these nanoparticles play a crucial role in determining their behavior throughout the cement hydration process, thereby impacting the mechanical properties of the resulting nanocomposites. Qdp enhances the controlled and beneficial interaction with the cementitious matrix by reducing excessive breakdown and gaseous product emission. Using localized heat to break up chemical interactions makes the mortar nanocomposites stronger, last longer, and keep their structure better. The examination of Qdl and Qdp's thermal characteristics reveals the underlying reasons for their different effects on the mechanical properties of the resulting cementitious materials. Moreover, the TGA curve demonstrates that there is no substantial disparity between Qdl and Qdp, suggesting that the chemical composition of the yielded cement products stays comparable. The inclusion of either Qdl or Qdp does not lead to the hydrated phase modification.

The enhanced mechanical and durability characteristics of GQD-ement composites can be primarily due to their structure in micro and atomic levels. The SEM-EDS analysis was conducted on hardened control, Qdl-1.2, and Qdp-1.2 cement composites to investigate their microstructural characteristics, as shown in Fig. 13 . The micrographs demonstrate the emergence of hydration products, characterized by distinct CSH gel and calcium hydroxide (CH) precipitation. When observed at a magnification of 6000×, the existence of interconnected needle-like CSH phases becomes apparent. GQDs act as secondary nucleation sites for CSH phases, enhancing the kinetics at which microcrystals develop. This can be conformed in typical matters when adding accelerators to the cement. This leads to a denser microstructure and a tighter boundary between different materials, hence improving the mechanical capabilities and long-term resilience. Furthermore, the micrographs did not reveal any microcracks. It is worth mentioning that similar observations were made for Qdl, as SEM micrographs showed similar characteristics to Qdp. In addition, the EDS spectra of several cement composites were examined. The findings, as shown in Table 10 , suggest that there were no significant differences in the chemical compositions of the control, Qdl, and Qdp mixes. The minimal amounts of additives utilized be a reason at which is not apparently observed in Raman spectroscopy analysis, as shown in Fig. 14 . The Raman spectroscopy can be used to detect and mapping the cement composites and carbon distribution [64]. The carbon component from GQDs is not seen; meaningly, the uniform dispersion of the Qdl and Qdp takes place throughout the cement matrix. None of agglomeration of GQDs is detected. Furthermore, while the previous studies [21] primarily focused on the individual effects of various carbon nanomaterials, this work presents a comparative analysis of different GQDs variations, offering a deeper understanding of how these materials can be optimized for specific performance enhancements in cement composites. The nano-bridging mechanism identified, facilitated by the secondary nucleation and crystallization of CSH gel, is also a novel finding that contributes to the body of knowledge on the microstructural effects of GQDs in cement composites.

This study explored the complex incorporation of GQDs (particularly Qdl and Qdp) into cement composites, covering concentrations ranging from 0.3 % to 1.2 % based on the mass of cement. An analysis of the GQDs contents added indicated significant effects on the thermal and physical characteristics as well as microstructural composition, which made them to be a conductive composites. The following findings are summarized below.
1)
As the Qdl content increased, the workability increased due to its natural hydrophilic properties. Also, the quasi-hydrophobic properties of the Qdp blends and the small amount of superplasticizer added to the Qdp solutions made them easier to work with.
2)
The Qdl and Qdp composites exhibited significant improvements in their physical attributes compared to the control group.
3)
Qdl and Qdp were added, resulting in an increase in compressive and flexural strengths. Among the composites tested, the one with 0.3 % Qdl showed the best performance. In terms of mechanics, the improvements were made possible by GQDs's great ability to mix with water. This let a lot of functional groups interact with each other in the bonding of cement hydration products. This resulted in the nano-level matrix compaction and connection. Furthermore, there was a noticeable gain in mechanical properties as the amount of Qdp grew. This can be attributed to its strong physical strength and the fact that it forms fewer bonds with cement hydration products.
4)
The composites of GQDs demonstrated enhanced thermal characteristics, including surface temperature, thermal conductivity, thermal diffusivity, and specific heat capacity. This indicates the ability to diminish early-age thermal cracking and enhance the durability of concrete structures, making GQDs composites beneficial for specific applications.
5)
The BET analysis showed that adding Qdl and Qdp made the pore structure more refined. This meant that the diameters of nano-, meso-, and micro-pores got smaller, and the overall volume of pores got smaller as well. This feature is due to its low permeability and good quality. The TGA data highlighted the significant impact of Qdp on cement's primary hydrates, thereby improving hydration reactions.
6)
A study using scanning electron microscopy showed that the CSH gel in the cement paste got denser after the addition of Qdl and Qdp. This was shown by the nano-bridging mechanisms seen in the CSH structure. The small size of the particles in the mortar promotes the formation of CSH gel and CH on the surface of GQDs in cement mortar. This leads to enhanced compressive and flexural strength values, as well as improved thermal characteristics.

Ongoing research efforts focus on creating large-scale projects to assess their thermal conductivity as well as their suitability for explicit functions for a smart city project, such as energy autarkic buildings and self-charging pavements for electric vehicles, to intermittent energy storage foundations for green wind turbines and tidal power stations.

Thwe Thwe Win: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Natthaphon Raengthon: Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Lapyote Prasittisopin: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
